item.title: Digest #197: AI in DevOps, AWS Security Vulnerabilities and Terraform Reviewer Agent
item.author: Mohamed Labouardy
item.content:encoded: Welcome to this week’s edition of the DevOps Bulletin.AI isn’t replacing DevOps teams, it’s changing how they work. Learn how by joining the IaCConf Spotlight on January 28. In the news, AWS’s direction in 2026 comes under scrutiny, a critical unauthenticated RCE in n8n puts thousands of instances at risk, and Wiz uncovered a way attackers could hijack AWS accounts and GitHub repos through CodeBuild. We also cover thousands of secrets leaked via online code formatters, growing concerns around GitHub’s monopoly in open source, and why attention fragmentation (not bad AI code) may be the biggest productivity killer for engineers this year.On the hands-on side: running projects in dev containers, rebuilding a Talos cluster from bare metal, safely limiting AI coding agents’ access to secrets, improving AI SRE workflows with better observability, and a curated set of Linux and open-source tutorials worth bookmarking. There’s also a beginner-friendly Python guide for those starting fresh in 2026.Open-source picks this week include Snitch, a human-friendly netstat replacement; Leash, a security wrapper for running AI coding agents in locked-down containers; a CLI and MCP server for managing Lambda GPU instances; a lab of intentionally vulnerable MCP servers for learning AI agent security failures; and tusk-drift-cli, which replays real production traffic as deterministic API tests.All this and more in this week’s DevOps Bulletin, don’t miss out!AI isn’t replacing your team. It’s changing how they work.Learn how platform and DevOps teams are using AI safely at scale at IaCConf Spotlight on January 28 - Consider supporting it with a paid subscription. You’ll keep the free Friday issues get extras like bonus deep-dives, templates, and the full archive.A that runs AI coding agents in containers and enforces real-time policies through full system monitoring.An unofficial and MCP server that lets humans and AI assistants manage Lambda cloud GPU instances.A hands-on of intentionally vulnerable MCP servers for learning AI agent and tool security failures.A that replays real production traffic as deterministic API tests for local and CI environments. If you have feedback to share or are interested in sponsoring this newsletter, feel free to reach out via or simply reply to this email.

item.title: Amazon EC2 X8i instances powered by custom Intel Xeon 6 processors are generally available for memory-intensive workloads
item.author: Channy Yun (윤석찬)
item.content:encoded: Since a preview launch at AWS re:Invent 2025, we’re announcing the general availability of new memory-optimized Amazon Elastic Compute Cloud (Amazon EC2) X8i instances. These instances are powered by custom Intel Xeon 6 processors with a sustained all-core turbo frequency of 3.9 GHz, available only on AWS. These SAP certified instances deliver the highest performance and fastest memory bandwidth among comparable Intel processors in the cloud.X8i instances are ideal for memory-intensive workloads including in-memory databases such as SAP HANA, traditional large-scale databases, data analytics, and electronic design automation (EDA), which require high compute performance and a large memory footprint.These instances provide 1.5 times more memory capacity (up to 6 TB), and 3.4 times more memory bandwidth compared to previous generation X2i instances. These instances offer up to 43% higher performance compared to X2i instances, with higher gains on some of the real-world workloads. They deliver up to 50% higher SAP Application Performance Standard (SAPS) performance, up to 47% faster PostgreSQL performance, up to 88% faster Memcached performance, and up to 46% faster AI inference performance.During the preview, customers like RISE with SAP utilized up to 6 TB of memory capacity with 50% higher compute performance compared to X2i instances. This enabled faster transaction processing and improved query response times for SAP HANA workloads. Orion reduced the number of active cores on X8i instances compared to X2idn instances while maintaining performance thresholds, cutting SQL Server licensing costs by 50%. X8i instances are available in 14 sizes including three larger instance sizes (, , and ), so you can choose the right size for your application to scale up, and two bare metal sizes ( and ) to deploy workloads that benefit from direct access to physical resources. X8i instances feature up to 100 Gbps of network bandwidth with support for the Elastic Fabric Adapter (EFA) and up to 80 Gbps of throughput to Amazon Elastic Block Store (Amazon EBS).Here are the specs for X8i instances:X8i instances support the instance bandwidth configuration (IBC) feature like other eighth-generation instance types, offering flexibility to allocate resources between network and EBS bandwidth. You can scale network or EBS bandwidth by up to 25%, improving database performance, query processing speeds, and logging efficiency. These instances also use sixth-generation AWS Nitro cards, which offload CPU virtualization, storage, and networking functions to dedicated hardware and software, enhancing performance and security for your workloads. Amazon EC2 X8i instances are now available in US East (N. Virginia), US East (Ohio), US West (Oregon), and Europe (Frankfurt) AWS Regions. For Regional availability and a future roadmap, search the instance type in the resources tab of AWS Capabilities by Region.

item.title: CNL: The CNCF Annual Cloud Native Survey — Infrastructure for AI’s Future
item.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
item.content:encoded:
Join us for a live discussion unpacking the findings from, The CNCF Annual Cloud Native Survey: The Infrastructure of AI's Future, the CNCF Annual Report built from open, community-sourced data across industries and regions.
Hilary Carter, SVP of Research at the Linux Foundation, Jan-Erik Aase, Partner and Global Head of ISG Provider Lens, and Jeffrey Sica , Head of Projects at CNCF, will explore what the data reveals about Kubernetes in production, infrastructure maturity, and the evolving role of people and culture in cloud native and AI adoption.
Hear expert insights, real-world implications, and what these trends mean for the future of modern infrastructure. The live session will be followed by a Q&A, so bring your questions!

item.title: From AI agent prototype to product: Lessons from building AWS DevOps Agent
item.author: Efe Karakus
item.content:encoded: At re:Invent 2025, Matt Garman announced AWS DevOps Agent, a frontier agent that resolves and proactively prevents incidents, continuously improving reliability and performance. As a member of the DevOps Agent team, we’ve focused heavily on making sure that the “incident response” capability of the DevOps Agent generates useful findings and observations. In particular, we’ve been working on making root cause analysis for native AWS applications accurate and performant. Under the hood, DevOps Agent has a multi-agent architecture where a lead agent acts as an incident commander: it understands the symptom, creates an investigation plan, and delegates individual tasks to specialized sub-agents when those tasks benefit from context compression. A sub-agent executes its task with a pristine context window and reports compressed results back to the lead agent. For example, when examining high-volume log records, a sub-agent filters through the noise to surface only relevant messages to the lead agent.In this blog post, we want to focus on the mechanisms one needs to develop to build an agentic product that works. Building a prototype with large language models (LLMs) has a low barrier to entry – you can showcase something that works fairly quickly. However, graduating that prototype into a product that performs reliably across diverse customer environments is a different challenge entirely, and one that is frequently underestimated. This post shares what we learned building AWS DevOps Agent so you can apply these lessons to your own agent development.In our experience, there are five mechanisms necessary to continuously improve agent quality and bridge the gap from prototype to production. First, you need to identify where your agent fails and where it can improve, while establishing a quality baseline for the types of scenarios your agent handles well. Second, you need a to debug agent trajectories and understand where exactly the agent went wrong. Third, you need a with the ability to rerun those failing scenarios locally to iterate. Fourth, you need to make : establishing success criteria before modifying your system to avoid confirmation bias. Finally, you need to regularly to understand actual customer experience and discover new scenarios your evals don’t yet cover.Evals are the machine learning equivalent of a test suite in traditional software engineering. Just like building any other software product, a collection of good test cases builds confidence in quality. Iterating on agent quality is similar to test-driven development (TDD): you have an eval scenario that the agent fails (the test is red), you make changes until the agent passes (the test is green). A passing eval means the agent arrived at an accurate, useful output through correct reasoning.For AWS DevOps Agent, the size of an individual eval scenario is similar to an end-to-end test in the traditional software engineering testing pyramid. Looking through the lens of “Given-When-Then” style tests: – Once the fault is injected, an alarm fires, and this triggers the AWS DevOps Agent to start its investigation. The eval framework polls the records that the Agent generates, just like how the DevOps Agent web application renders them. This section isn’t fundamentally different from defining the action in an integration or end-to-end test. – This asserts and reports on multiple metrics. Fundamentally, there’s a single “PASS” (1) or “FAIL” (0) metric for quality. For the DevOps Agent’s incident response capability, a “PASS” means the right root cause surfaced to the customer – in our example, this means identifying the faulty deployment as the root cause and tracing the dependency chain to surface the impacted resources and observations that reveal the missing S3 write permission; otherwise “FAIL”. We define this as a : not just “did the agent find the root cause?” but “did the agent arrive at the root cause through the correct reasoning with the right supporting evidence?”The ground truth (the “expected” or “wanted” in software testing parlance) is compared to the system response (the “actual”) via an LLM Judge – an LLM that receives both the ground truth and the agent’s actual output, then emits its reasoning and a verdict on whether they match. We use an LLM for comparison because the agent’s output is non-deterministic: the agent follows an overall output format but generates the actual text freely, so each run may use different words or sentence structures while conveying the same semantic meaning. We don’t want to strictly search for keywords in the final root cause analysis report but rather evaluate whether the essence of the rubric is met.The evaluation report is structured with scenarios as rows and metrics as columns. Key metrics that we keep track of are capability (pass@k – whether the agent passed at least once in k attempts), reliability (pass^k – how many times the agent passed across k attempts, e.g., 0.33 means passed 1 out of 3 times for k=3), latency, and token usage.There are several benefits to having evals:Red scenarios provide obvious investigation points for the agent development team to increase product quality.Over time, green scenarios act as regression tests, notifying us when changes to the system degrade the existing customer experience.Once pass rates are green, we can improve customer experience along additional metrics. For example, reducing end-to-end latency and/or optimizing cost (proxied by token usage) while maintaining the quality bar.What makes evals challenging?There are several challenges with evals. In decreasing order of difficulty:Realistic and diverse scenarios are hard to author. Coming up with realistic applications and fault scenarios is difficult. Authoring high fidelity microservice applications and faults is significant work that requires prior industry experience. What we’ve found effective: we author a few “environments” (based on real application architectures) but create failure scenarios on top of them. The environment is the expensive portion of the evaluation setup, so we maximize reuse across multiple scenarios. If the “Given” takes 20 minutes to deploy for an eval scenario and then the “When” takes another 10-20 minutes for complex investigations to complete, agent developers won’t thoroughly test their changes. Instead, they’ll be satisfied with a single passing eval, then release to production, potentially introducing regressions until the comprehensive eval report is generated. Additionally, slow feedback loops encourages batching multiple changes together rather than small incremental experiments, making it harder to understand which change actually moved the needle. We’ve found three mechanisms effective for speeding up feedback loops: Long-running environments for eval scenarios. The application and its healthy state are created once and kept running. Fault injection happens periodically (e.g., over weekends), and developers point their agent credentials at the faulty environment, completely skipping the “Given” portion of the test. of only the agent surface area that matters. In our multi-agent system, developers can trigger a specific sub-agent directly with a prompt from a past eval run rather than running the entire end-to-end flow. Additionally, we built a “fork” feature: developers can initialize any agent with the conversation history from a failing run up to a specific checkpoint message, then iterate only on the remaining trajectory. Both of these approaches significantly lowers the wait time of the “When” portion.of the agentic system. If developers must merge changes and release to a cloud environment before testing, the loop is too slow. Running locally enables rapid iteration.When an agent fails an eval or a production run, where do you start investigating? The most productive method is error analysis. Visualize the agent’s complete trajectory, every user-assistant message exchange including sub-agent trajectories, and annotate each step as “PASS” or “FAIL” with notes on what went wrong. This process is tedious but effective.For AWS DevOps Agent, agent trajectories map to OpenTelemetry traces and you can use tools like Jaeger to visualize them. Software development kits like Strands provide tracing integration with minimal setup.Figure 1 – A sample trace from AWS DevOps Agent.Each span contains user-assistant message pairs. We annotate each span’s quality in a table such as the following:This low-level analysis consistently surfaces multiple improvements, not just one. For a single failing eval, one will typically identify many concrete changes spanning accuracy, performance, and cost.You’ve identified failing scenarios and diagnosed the issues through trajectory analysis. Now it’s time to modify the system.The biggest fallacy we’ve observed at this stage: leading to. Given the eval challenges mentioned earlier (slow feedback loops and the impracticality of comprehensive test suites) developers typically test only the few specific failing scenarios locally until they pass. One modifies the context (system prompt, tool specifications, tool implementations, etc.) until one or two scenarios pass, without considering broader impact. When changes don’t follow context engineering best practices, they likely have negative effects that we can’t capture through limited evals.Establish success criteria firstBefore making any change, define what success looks like: Fix specific git commit IDs for the current system. Think deliberately about which metrics would improve both the agent’s experience and the customer’s experience, then gather those metrics for the baseline. Which evals will measure your change’s impact? How many times will you rerun these evals? Convince yourself this set represents broader customer patterns, not just the one failure you’re investigating. Measure your changes against the baseline using the same metrics.This intentional framing protects against confirmation bias (interpreting results favorably) and sunk cost fallacy (accepting changes simply because you invested time). If your modifications don’t move the metrics as expected, reject them.For example, when optimizing a within AWS DevOps Agent, we establish a baseline by fixing git commit IDs and running the same scenario seven times. This reveals both typical performance and variance.Each metric measures a different dimension:Correct observations – How many signals (log records, metric data, code snippets, etc.) that are directly related to the incident did the sub-agent surface?Irrelevant observations – How much did the sub-agent introduce to the lead agent? This counts signals that are unrelated to the incident and could distract the agent’s investigation.Latency – How long did the sub-agent take (measured in minutes and seconds)?Sub-agent tokens – How many tokens did the sub-agent to accomplish its task? This serves as a proxy for the cost of running the sub-agent.Lead-agent tokens – How much of the lead agent’s context window is the sub-agent’s input and output consuming? This gives us a tangible way to identify optimization opportunities for the sub-agent tool: can we compress the instructions to the sub-agent or the results it returns?After establishing the baseline, we compare these metrics against the same measurements with our proposed changes. This makes it clear whether the change is an actual improvement.We’ve been fortunate to have several Amazon teams adopt AWS DevOps Agent early. A DevOps agent team member on rotation regularly samples real production runs using our trajectory visualization tool (similar to the OpenTelemetry-based visualization discussed earlier, but customized to render DevOps Agent-specific artifacts like root cause analysis reports and observations), marking whether the agent’s output was accurate and identifying failure points. Production samples are irreplaceable; they reveal the actual customer experience. Additionally, reviewing samples continuously refines your intuition of what the agent is good and bad at. When production runs aren’t satisfactory, you have real-world scenarios to iterate against: modify your agent locally, then rerun it against the same production environment until the desired outcome is reached. Establishing rapport with a few critical early adopter teams willing to partner in this way is invaluable. They provide ground truth for rapid iteration and create opportunities to identify new eval scenarios. This tight feedback loop with production data works in conjunction with eval-driven development to form a comprehensive test suite.Building an agent prototype that demonstrates the feasibility of solving a real business problem is an exciting first step. The harder work is graduating that prototype into a product that performs reliably across diverse customer environments and tasks. In this post, we’ve shared five mechanisms that form the foundation for systematically improving agent quality: evals with realistic and diverse scenarios, fast feedback loops, trajectory visualization, intentional changes, and production sampling.If you’re building an agentic application, start building your eval suite today. Even starting with a handful critical scenarios will establish the quality baseline needed to measure and improve systematically. To see how AWS DevOps Agent applies these principles to incident response, check out our getting started guide.

item.title: Permission-Aware RAG: End-to-End Testing with the SpiceDB Testcontainer
item.author: Jennifer Kohl
item.content:encoded:
We use GenAI in every facet of technology now – internal knowledge bases, customer support systems, and code review bots, to name just a few use cases. And in nearly every one of these, someone eventually asks:This is a roadblock that companies building RAG features or AI Agents eventually hit – the moment where an LLM returns data from a document that the user was not authorized to access, introducing potential legal, financial, and reputational risk to all parties. Unfortunately, traditional methods of authorization are not suited for the hierarchical, dynamic nature of access control in RAG. This is exactly where modern authorization permissioning systems such as shine: in building fine-grained authorization for filtering content in your AI-powered applications.In fact, OpenAI uses SpiceDB to secure 37 Billion documents for 5 Million users who use ChatGPT Connectors – a feature where you bring your data from different sources such as Google Drive, Dropbox, GitHub etc. into ChatGPT.This blog post shows how you can pair SpiceDB with to give you the ability to test your permission logic inside your RAG pipeline, end-to-end, automatically, with zero infrastructure dependencies.The example repo can be found hereQuick Primer on AuthorizationBefore diving into implementation, let’s clarify two foundational concepts: (verifying a user is) and (deciding they can access).Authorization is commonly implemented via techniques such as:Access Control Lists (ACLs)Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC)However, for complex, dynamic, and context-rich applications like RAG pipelines, traditional methods such as RBAC or ABAC fall short. The new kid on the block – (Relationship-Based Access Control) is ideal as it models access as a graph of relationships rather than fixed rules, providing the necessary flexibility and scalability required.ReBAC was popularized in Google Zanzibar, the internal authorization system Google built to manage permissions across all its products (e.g., Google Docs, Drive). Zanzibar systems are optimized for low-latency, high-throughput authorization checks, and global consistency – requirements that are well-suited for RAG systems. is the most scalable open-source implementation of Google’s Zanzibar authorization model. It stores access as a relationship graph, where the fundamental check reduces to: For a Google Docs-style example:definition user {} definition document { relation reader: user relation writer: user
permission read = reader + writer permission write = writer }
This schema defines object types ( and ), explicit between the objects (, ), and derived (, ). SpiceDB evaluates the relationship graph in microseconds, enabling real-time authorization checks at massive scale.RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) is an architectural pattern that enhances Large Language Models (LLMs) by letting them consult an external knowledge base, typically involving a component finding document chunks and the LLM generating an informed response.This pattern is now used by businesses and enterprises for apps like chatbots that query sensitive data such as customer playbooks or PII – all stored in a vector database for performance. However, the fundamental risk in this flow is : the Retriever component ignores permissions, and the LLM will happily summarize unauthorized data. In fact, OWASP has a Top 10 Risks for Large Language Model Applications list which includes Sensitive Information Disclosure, Excessive Agency & Vector and Embedding Weaknesses. The consequences of this leakage can be severe, ranging from loss of customer trust to massive financial and reputational damage from compliance violations.This setup desperately needs fine-grained authorization, and that’s where comes in. SpiceDB can post-filter retrieved documents by performing real-time authorization checks, ensuring the model only uses data the querying user is permitted to see. The only requirement is that the documents have that indicates where the information came from.But testing this critical permission logic without mocks, manual Docker setup, or flaky Continuous Integration (CI) environments is tricky. provides the perfect solution, allowing you to spin up a real, production-grade, and disposable SpiceDB instance inside your unit tests to deterministically verify that your RAG pipeline respects permissions end-to-end.Spin Up Real Authorization for Every TestInstead of mocking your authorization system or manually running it on your workstation, you can add this line of code in your test:container, _ := spicedbcontainer.Run(ctx, "authzed/spicedb:v1.47.1") Pull the real SpiceDB imageStart it in a clean, isolated environmentHand you the gRPC endpointBecause Testcontainers handles the full lifecycle – from pulling the container, exposing dynamic ports, and tearing it down automatically, you eliminate manual processes such as running Docker commands, and writing cleanup scripts. This isolation ensures that every single test runs with a fresh, clean authorization graph, preventing data conflicts, and making your permission tests in your IDE and across parallel Continuous Integration (CI) builds.Suddenly you have a real, production-grade, Zanzibar-style permissions engine inside your unit test. Using SpiceDB & TestcontainersHere’s a walkthrough of how you can achieve end-to-end permissions testing using SpiceDB and Testcontainers. The source code for this tutorial can be found here.For the sake of simplicity, we have a minimal RAG and the retrieval mechanism is trivial too. We’re going to test three documents which have doc_ids ( ..) that act as metadata. Charlie (or anyone) can view This SpiceDB schema defines a and a object type. A user has permission on a document if they are the direct or the of the document.definition user {}
definition document { relation owner: user relation viewer: user | owner permission read = owner + viewer } 2. Starting the Testcontainer Here’s how a line of code can start a test to launch the disposable SpiceDB instance:container, err := spicedbcontainer.Run(ctx, "authzed/spicedb:v1.47.1") require.NoError(t, err) Next, we connect to the running containerized service:host, _ := container.Host(ctx) port, _ := container.MappedPort(ctx, "50051/tcp") endpoint := fmt.Sprintf("%s:%s", host, port.Port())
client, err := authzed.NewClient( endpoint, grpc.WithTransportCredentials(insecure.NewCredentials()), grpcutil.WithInsecureBearerToken("somepresharedkey"), )
This is now a fully-functional SpiceDB instance running inside your test runner.3. Load the Schema + Test DataThe test seeds data the same way your application would:_, err := client.WriteSchema(ctx, &apiv1.WriteSchemaRequest{Schema: schema}) require.NoError(t, err) rel("document", "doc1", "owner", "user", "emilia") rel("document", "doc2", "viewer", "user", "beatrice") rel("document", "doc3", "viewer", "user", "emilia") rel("document", "doc3", "viewer", "user", "beatrice") rel("document", "doc3", "viewer", "user", "charlie") We now have a predictable, reproducible authorization graph for every test run.4. Post-Filtering With SpiceDBBefore the LLM sees anything, we check permissions with SpiceDB which acts as the source of truth of the permissions in the documents.resp, err := r.spiceClient.CheckPermission(ctx, &apiv1.CheckPermissionRequest{ Resource: docObject, Permission: "read", Subject: userSubject, }) If SpiceDB says , the doc is never fed into the LLM, thereby ensuring the user gets an answer to their query only based on what they have permissions to read.Overly permissive vector searchTraditional access controls break down when data becomes embeddings hence having guardrails prevents this from happening. End-to-End Permission Checks in a Single TestHere’s what the full test asserts:Emilia queries “roadmap” → gets Because they’re the owner.Beatrice queries “playbook” → gets Because she’s a viewer.Charlie queries “public” → gets Because it’s the only doc he can read, as it’s a public docIf there is a single failing permission rule, the end-to-end test will immediately fail, which is critical given the constant changes in RAG pipelines (such as new retrieval modes, embeddings, document types, or permission rules). What If Your RAG Pipeline Isn’t in Go?What if your RAG pipeline is written in a different language such as Python? Not to worry, there’s also a community Testcontainers module written in Python that you can use similarly. The module can be found here.Typically, you would integrate it in your integration tests like this: # Your RAG pipeline test def test_rag_pipeline_respects_permissions(): with SpiceDBContainer() as spicedb: # Set up permissions schema client = create_spicedb_client( spicedb.get_endpoint(), spicedb.get_secret_key() )
# Load your permissions model client.WriteSchema(your_document_permission_schema)
# Write test relationships # User A can access Doc 1 # User B can access Doc 2
# Test RAG pipeline with User A results = rag_pipeline.search(query="...", user="A") assert "Doc 1" in results assert "Doc 2" not in results # Should be filtered out!
Similar to the Go module, this container gives you a clean, isolated SpiceDB instance for every test run.Why This Approach MattersAuthorization testing in RAG pipelines can be tricky, given the scale and latency requirement and it can get trickier in systems handling sensitive data. By integrating the flexibility and scale of SpiceDB with the automated, isolated environments of Testcontainers, you shift to a completely reliable, approach to authorization. Every time your code ships, a fresh, production-grade authorization engine is spun up, loaded with test data, and torn down cleanly, guaranteeing zero drift between your development machine and CI. This pattern can ensure that your RAG system is safe, correct, and permission-aware as it scales from three documents to millions.

item.title: KodeKloud AWS Challenge — Day 22: Configuring Secure SSH Access to an EC2 Instance
item.author: Kishor Bhairat
item.content:encoded: KodeKloud AWS Challenge — Day 22: Configuring Secure SSH Access to an EC2 InstanceA hands-on implementation of passwordless SSH access using key-based authentication — how real teams securely access EC2 instances without exposing credentials.Day 22 of the KodeKloud AWS Challenge focused on secure SSH access to an EC2 instance from a trusted landing host (aws-client).This is not about “just logging in.” This is about eliminating passwords, controlling access paths, and enforcing key-based authentication — the baseline for any production-grade Linux infrastructure.Launch an EC2 instance named nautilus-ec2Generate an SSH key on the aws-client hostEnable by configuring authorized keys correctlyConcept Explanation: Secure SSH Access (How Professionals Do It)In production environments, password-based SSH is a liability. (public + private)Private key stays on the clientPublic key is stored on the server in ~/.ssh/authorized_keysWhen a connection is initiated:The client proves possession of the private keyThe server verifies it against the public keyAccess is granted — no password exchangeThis is faster, safer, and auditable.Why This Matters in Real EnvironmentsNever allow SSH passwordsRestrict access to Rotate keys instead of passwordsLog and monitor access pathsaws-client acts as a EC2 only trusts keys coming from that hostAttack surface is drastically reducedThis is standard operating procedure — not an advanced trick.Common mistakes engineers make here:Incorrect permissions on .ssh directoriesWrong ownership of authorized_keysForgetting that SSH is Allowing SSH from 0.0.0.0/0Leaving password authentication enabledIf permissions are wrong, SSH will fail silently — and that’s by design.Hands-On Task: What I DidThe task followed a realistic infrastructure workflow:Checked for an existing SSH key under /root/.ssh/Generated a new SSH key pair if it didn’t existEnsured proper permissions on:On the EC2 instance (nautilus-ec2)Launched a Linux EC2 instance:Logged in with initial accessAdded the from aws-client to:/root/.ssh/authorized_keysVerified correct file ownership and permissionsConfirmed passwordless SSH access from aws-clientResult: secure, passwordless SSH access established exactly as required.What I Learned / Key TakeawaysSSH keys are mandatory for secure accessPasswordless SSH reduces attack vectorsPermissions matter as much as configurationBastion-style access improves security postureThis pattern scales cleanly across environmentsIf you still rely on SSH passwords, your infrastructure is behind.Configuring secure SSH access isn’t optional — it’s foundational.This challenge reinforced that access security is about trust boundaries, not convenience. By enforcing key-based authentication and limiting access to a known host, the Nautilus DevOps team established a secure and auditable access model.This is how production systems are accessed — quietly, securely, and intentionally.On to the next challenge.If you’re learning AWS or DevOps through hands-on labs that mirror real operational practices, follow the journey. Each day focuses on skills that actually matter in production.

item.title: KodeKloud AWS Challenge — Day 21: Launching an EC2 Instance with an Elastic IP
item.author: Kishor Bhairat
item.content:encoded: KodeKloud AWS Challenge — Day 21: Launching an EC2 Instance with an Elastic IPA hands-on walkthrough of deploying an EC2 instance with a stable public IP — covering why Elastic IPs matter in production AWS environments.Day 21 of the KodeKloud AWS Challenge focused on: launching an EC2 instance that must be reachable through a consistent public IP address.By default, EC2 public IPs are . Stop or restart the instance, and the IP changes. That’s unacceptable for applications that need a fixed endpoint. This is exactly why exist.The task was simple but critical:Launch an EC2 instance named nautilus-ec2Allocate and associate an Elastic IP named nautilus-eipConcept Explanation: Elastic IPs (Why They Exist)An is a static, public IPv4 address designed for dynamic cloud infrastructure.Remains the same even if the instance is stopped or restartedCan be remapped to another instance during failureAllocated to your AWS account, not tied permanently to one EC2 instanceIn real-world environments, Elastic IPs are used for:Public-facing applicationsAPIs with hardcoded endpointsLegacy systems expecting fixed IPsQuick failover between instancesIf you rely on default EC2 public IPs for production, you’re building on sand.Important points engineers often overlook:Elastic IPs are to a running instanceUnused EIPs incur chargesElastic IP ≠ High Availability by itselfFor scalable architectures, are preferred over EIPsElastic IPs solve stability — not scale.Hands-On Task: What I DidThe task was executed using standard AWS best practices:Logged in to the AWS Management ConsoleNavigated to Launched a new EC2 instance with:Completed instance launch and verified instance stateNavigated to Allocated a new Elastic IPAssociated the Elastic IP with nautilus-ec2Named the Elastic IP as nautilus-eipVerified that the instance now had a stable public IPThis is the standard workflow for ensuring reliable external access to EC2 workloads.What I Learned / Key TakeawaysDefault EC2 public IPs are Elastic IPs provide EIPs are ideal for single-instance workloadsCost awareness matters — unused EIPs are billedFor scale, EIPs should be replaced with load balancersThis task reinforces infrastructure , not convenience.Launching an EC2 instance with an Elastic IP is a foundational AWS skill — but also a design decision.Elastic IPs solve the problem of , not high availability or scalability. Knowing when to use them — and when not to — is what separates cloud users from cloud engineers.This challenge highlighted how small configuration choices directly affect reliability in production systems.On to the next challenge.If you’re learning AWS or DevOps through hands-on challenges and real infrastructure scenarios, follow the journey.Each day builds practical skills that translate directly to real-world environments.

item.title: Managing Private PKI in Kubernetes with cert-manager
item.author: Rishabh Singh
item.content:encoded: Let’s be honest: configuring SSL and managing certificates is one of those tasks that feels manageable at first, but scales poorly. You start with one or two sites, and it’s no big deal. But as your environment grows, so does the complexity.I have personally navigated the chaos of managing dozens of websites, each with its own expiry all round the year. The earlier way of managing the life cycle of the certificate was a massive spreadsheet. Every new team member had to be on-boarded to this document, and every renewal required someone to remember to update it. As delivery velocity increased, this approach became fragile and error-prone.Kubernetes makes this problem even harder. Microservices, frequent deployments multiply the number of certificates that need to be created, rotated, and trusted. Manual tracking simply does not scale in such an environment. This is exactly the kind of operational problem Kubernetes operators were designed to solve.The primary value proposition is . Once you configure your Certificate Authority (CA) stack, cert-manager handles the heavy lifting. Once you’ve set up your Certificate Authority (CA) stack, cert-manager basically puts your certificate lifecycle on autopilot. At creation time, cert-manager even schedules renewals using a renewalTime field, ensuring certificates are rotated without human intervention.When approaching certificate management, organizations generally fall into two camps: Industry-standard authorities (like Let’s Encrypt) whose root certificates are pre-installed in browser trust bundles. Authorities used within an organization for internal service-to-service communication. These require the CA certificate to be manually added to the trust stores of all internal clients and applications.While cert-manager integrates seamlessly with public authorities like Let’s Encrypt via DNS-01 or HTTP-01 challenges, this article focuses on the . I’ll walk you through a practical use case for internally signed certificates, showing you how to bootstrap a self-signed chain (root and intermediate)and generate certificates via an intermediate CA.To follow the steps in this article, you will need a Kubernetes cluster with the cert-manager Operator installed.Creating a kind cluster is the most straight forward way of starting a Kubernetes cluster.You can utilize the static deployment of cert-manager for installation. Executing the following command to install the cert-manager operator in your Kubernetes cluster:kubectl apply -f https://github.com/cert-manager/cert-manager/releases/download/vxxxx/cert-manager.yamlBoot Strapping Certificate AuthoritiesThere are two ways to bootstrap a certificate authority in cert-manager. In this article, we will use a certificate chain of Root → Intermediate → Server.Self-signed certificate chain using cert-managerThe most common way to start is by letting manage the entire chain of trust. This involves a “loop” pattern: we use a temporary self-signed issuer to create a Root CA, then use that Root CA to create an Intermediate CA, and finally use that Intermediate to issue our actual application certificates.This approach is highly robust because it creates a clear Root → Intermediate → Server hierarchy, following industry best practices for security isolation.Here is how we build this chain step-by-step:Create a ClusterIssuer object with spec.selfSigned.apiVersion: cert-manager.io/v1kind: ClusterIssuer name: selfsigned-cluster-issuer selfSigned: {}2. The first step configured self signed Cluster Issuer. Below we are generating the root certificate for the self signed ClusterIssuer created in the previous step. This Certificate object will create the certificate and store it in root-ca-key-pair secret.apiVersion: cert-manager.io/v1kind: Certificate name: selfsigned-root-caspec: commonName: selfsigned-root-ca-rishabh-authority secretName: root-ca-key-pair duration: 52560h # 6 years algorithm: ECDSA issuerRef: name: selfsigned-cluster-issuer kind: ClusterIssuer3. Bootstraping the root certificate authority: Configure the root-ca-key-pair in a ClusterIssuer under spec.ca. This will bootstrap our root certificate as a certificate authority.apiVersion: cert-manager.io/v1kind: ClusterIssuer name: ca-issuer ca: secretName: root-ca-key-pair4. Creating the intermediate certificate : Now that we have our root certificate authority we will generate the intermediate certificate using the root certificate authority created in previous step.apiVersion: cert-manager.io/v1kind: Certificate name: selfsigned-intermediate-caspec: commonName: selfsigned-intermediate-ca-rishabh-authority # Note this secretName: intermediate-ca-key-pair #----(2) privateKey: size: 256 duration: 17520h # 2 year issuerRef: name: ca-issuer # match (1) kind: ClusterIssuer5. Bootstrapping intermediate certificate as Certificate authority : Since we want a certificate chain of root → intermediate → server, we must also configure the intermediate certificate as a certificate authority.apiVersion: cert-manager.io/v1kind: ClusterIssuer name: intermediate-ca-issuer ca: secretName: intermediate-ca-key-pair6. This is where we have created the chain of root and intermediate certificate. We will create following Certificate object, in order to create server certificate signed by the certificate chain.apiVersion: cert-manager.io/v1kind: Certificate name: api-cert-certificatespec: commonName: "*.example.com" # change me - "test.example.com" # change me - server auth kind: ClusterIssuer name: intermediate-ca-issuer secretName: api-certOn creating the above mentioned Certificate the signed certificate and associated private key will be stored in api-cert secret within default namespace.$ kubectl get secret -n default api-certNAME TYPE DATA AGEapi-cert kubernetes.io/tls 3 4d23h$ kubectl get secret -n default api-cert -oyamlapiVersion: v1 ca.crt: xxxxx tls.key: xxxxx==metadata: cert-manager.io/alt-names: test.example.com cert-manager.io/certificate-name: api-cert-certificate cert-manager.io/common-name: '*.example.com' cert-manager.io/ip-sans: "" cert-manager.io/issuer-group: "" cert-manager.io/issuer-kind: ClusterIssuer cert-manager.io/issuer-name: intermediate-ca-issuer cert-manager.io/uri-sans: "" creationTimestamp: "2026-01-08T09:58:08Z" controller.cert-manager.io/fao: "true" namespace: default uid: 730d8284-fd3f-420a-a508-6db0aebfe5c5# Parsing the subject of the server and the intermediate certificate$ kubectl get secret api-cert -n default -o json \| jq -r '.data["tls.crt"]' \| awk ' /BEGIN CERTIFICATE/ {cert=""} {cert = cert $0 "\n"} print "-----" print cert | "openssl x509 -noout -subject -ext subjectAltName" close("openssl x509 -noout -subject -ext subjectAltName")subject=CN = *.example.comX509v3 Subject Alternative Name: DNS:test.example.comsubject=CN = selfsigned-intermediate-ca-rishabh-authority# Parsing the subject of the root certificate$ kubectl get secret api-cert -n default -o json\| jq -r '.data["ca.crt"]' \| base64 -d \| openssl x509 -noout -subject -ext subjectAltNamesubject=CN = selfsigned-root-ca-rishabh-authorityUsing an existing certificate authorityThere are situations where you already have an internal certificate authority and do not want to bootstrap the entire certificate chain using cert-manager. In such cases, you may already have a root certificate or an intermediate certificate that you want to use for signing new certificates.For example, an organization might operate a central internal root CA and create a dedicated intermediate CA specifically for cert-manager to issue certificates for Kubernetes workloads.Lets bootstrap a root and intermediate certificate using the linked script. This script will create following directory structure. We will need the intermediate certificate and key from intermediate/certs/intermediate.cert.pem and intermediate/private/intermediate.key.pem . And in order to configure Certificate authority of intermediate certificate we also need to extract the root certificate from root/certs/ca.cert.pem$ tree pki/├── intermediate│ │ ├── ca-chain.cert.pem│ │ └── intermediate.cert.pem│ ├── crl│ ├── csr│ │ └── intermediate.csr.pem│ ├── index.txt│ ├── openssl.cnf│ │ └── intermediate.key.pem└── root │ └── ca.cert.pem ├── index.txt ├── index.txt.old │ └── 1000.pem ├── private ├── serialPopulate the secret with the intermediate certificate, its private key, and the root CA certificate. This secret will be referenced by the ClusterIssuer. The base64-encoded intermediate certificate should be stored in tls.crt, the private key in tls.key, and the root certificate in ca.crt.apiVersion: v1metadata: namespace: sandbox tls.crt: `REPLACE WITH cat intermediate/certs/intermediate.cert.pem | base64 -wo` tls.key: `REPLACE WITH cat intermediate/private/intermediate.key.pem | base64 -wo` ca.crt: `REPLACE WITH cat root/certs/ca.cert.pem | base64 -wo`2. Now we create the ClusterIssuer of type spec.ca . The spec.ca.secretName will point to the secret created in theprevious step.apiVersion: cert-manager.io/v1kind: ClusterIssuer name: detached-ca-issuer ca:3. Now we can generate the server certificate using this newly created ClusterIssuer. Here the Certificate Authority is created external to cert-manager.apiVersion: cert-manager.io/v1kind: Certificate name: api-cert-certificte-detachedspec: commonName: "*.example.com" # change me - "test.example.com" # change me - server auth kind: ClusterIssuer secretName: api-cert-detachedOn creating the above mentioned Certificate the signed certificate and associated private key will be stored in api-cert-detached secret within default namespace. You can now use this secret to enable SSL/TLS for your application workloads.$ kubectl get secret -n default api-cert-detached NAME TYPE DATA AGEapi-cert-detached kubernetes.io/tls 3 4d22h# Parsing the subject of the server and the intermediate certificate$ kubectl get secret api-cert-detached -n default -o json \| jq -r '.data["tls.crt"]' \| base64 -d \ /BEGIN CERTIFICATE/ {cert=""} /END CERTIFICATE/ { print cert | "openssl x509 -noout -subject -ext subjectAltName" close("openssl x509 -noout -subject -ext subjectAltName") }'subject=CN = *.example.comX509v3 Subject Alternative Name: DNS:test.example.comsubject=C = IN, ST = State, O = MyOrg, CN = IntermediateCA# Parsing the subject of the root certificate$ kubectl get secret api-cert-detached -n default -o json\| jq -r '.data["ca.crt"]' \| base64 -d \| openssl x509 -noout -subject -ext subjectAltNamesubject=C = IN, ST = State, L = City, O = MyOrg, CN = RootCAIn this article, we walked through both sides of private PKI management with cert-manager.First, we saw how to bootstrap an entire certificate chain — root, intermediate, and server certificates — using cert-manager itself. This approach is ideal when you want Kubernetes to own your complete PKI lifecycle.We then looked at the alternative model, where an organization already operates a root or intermediate CA outside the cluster, and cert-manager is used purely as a secure, automated certificate issuer on top of that existing trust hierarchy.By offloading the “operational learning” to the cert-manager operator, you eliminate the risk of human error. What cert-manager gives you is not just automation, but a way to make certificates first-class citizens of your cluster — versioned, reconciled, and continuously kept in a valid state.

item.title: Using OPA Gatekeeper to enforce read-only for kubectl diff
item.author: tjtharrison

item.title: KodeKloud AWS Challenge — Day 20: Creating an IAM Role for EC2 and Attaching a Policy
item.author: Kishor Bhairat
item.content:encoded: KodeKloud AWS Challenge — Day 20: Creating an IAM Role for EC2 and Attaching a PolicyA hands-on walkthrough of why IAM roles are mandatory for secure, scalable AWS infrastructure — and how EC2 instances access AWS services the right way.Day 20 of the KodeKloud AWS Challenge focused on creating an IAM role for an EC2 service and attaching an existing policy — one of the most important IAM patterns in real-world AWS environments.If you’re still thinking IAM users + access keys are the way EC2 talks to AWS services, stop. That’s outdated, insecure, and a red flag in production reviews.This task reinforced how IAM roles replace static credentials and enforce secure, temporary access at scale.Concept Explanation: IAM Roles (The Correct Way for AWS Services)An is an AWS identity assumed by a trusted entity, not logged into like a user. EC2 (AWS service) Allow EC2 instances to access AWS resources securely Temporary credentials issued by AWS STSRoles Roles Roles are by servicesThis is how AWS expects infrastructure to authenticate in Amazon Web Services.Why IAM Roles Matter in Real EnvironmentsIAM roles solve multiple production problems at once:No hardcoded credentials on serversAutomatic credential rotationFine-grained, auditable permissionsEasy permission updates without redeploying instancesCommon real-world use cases:EC2 publishing logs to CloudWatchEC2 reading secrets from Secrets ManagerEC2 interacting with other AWS APIsIf an EC2 instance needs AWS access, it should always use a role.Before creating the role, a few critical points:The role must trust the The policy must already exist (iampolicy_anita)Attaching the wrong policy = instant over-permissionIAM changes are Roles grant permissions to , not usersMisconfigured trust policies are one of the most common IAM mistakes.Hands-On Task: What I DidThe task requirements were precise and realistic: iamrole_anita iampolicy_anitaLogged in to the AWS Management ConsoleChose as the trusted entitySelected as the use caseAttached the existing policy Named the role Created and verified the role successfullyThis is the exact workflow used when preparing EC2 roles in production accounts.What I Learned / Key TakeawaysIAM roles are for EC2-to-AWS accessRoles eliminate static credentials entirelyPolicies define the role can doTrust policies define can assume the roleSecure infrastructure starts with correct IAM role designThis is baseline AWS security — not an advanced feature.Creating an IAM role for EC2 is not optional — it’s foundational.This challenge reinforced that secure AWS infrastructure depends on roles, not users, and that permissions must be intentionally scoped and service-specific.If your EC2 instances still use access keys, your IAM design is already broken.On to the next challenge — where IAM roles start interacting with real services.If you’re learning AWS or DevOps through hands-on labs and real IAM workflows, follow the journey. This series focuses on building skills that actually survive production environments.
